question_text: Two identical coils P and Q each of radius R are lying in perpendicular planes such that they have a common centre. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at the common centre when they carry currents equal to I and {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} I respectively.
explanation: {tex}\vec{B_{p}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}{/tex} , vertically upwards {tex}\vec{{B}_{Q}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}{/tex} , along with horizontal The resultant field at the centre is B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{P}^{2}+B_{Q}^{2}}=\left[\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}(1+3)^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{B P}{B Q}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o
solution: {tex}\vec{B_{p}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}{/tex} , vertically upwards {tex}\vec{{B}_{Q}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}{/tex} , along with horizontal The resultant field at the centre is B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{P}^{2}+B_{Q}^{2}}=\left[\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}(1+3)^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{B P}{B Q}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o

{tex}\vec{B_{p}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}{/tex}, vertically upwards
{tex}\vec{{B}_{Q}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}{/tex}, along with horizontal
The resultant field at the centre is
B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{P}^{2}+B_{Q}^{2}}=\left[\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}(1+3)^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{R}\right){/tex}
{tex}\tan \theta=\frac{B P}{B Q}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o

question_html:
1. Write Biot-Savart's law in vector form.
  2. Two identical circular coils A and B, each of radius R , carrying currents {tex}I{/tex} and {tex}\sqrt{3} I{/tex} respectively, are placed concentrically in XY and YZ planes respectively. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at their common centre.
question_text: Write Biot-Savart's law in vector form. Two identical circular coils A and B, each of radius R , carrying currents {tex}I{/tex} and {tex}\sqrt{3} I{/tex} respectively, are placed concentrically in XY and YZ planes respectively. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at their common centre.
explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow{{dB}}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}(\overrightarrow{{d}} \times \overrightarrow{{r}})}{{r}^3}{/tex} {tex} {B}_1 =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex} {tex} {~B}_2 =\frac{\mu_0 \sqrt{3} {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex} {tex} {~B} =\sqrt{{B}_1^2+{B}_2^2} {/tex} {tex} \therefore {~B} =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} \sqrt{1+3} {/tex} {tex} B =\frac{\mu_0 I}{R} {/tex} {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{B_1}{B_2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} {/tex} Direction of net magnetic field is {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} with direction of {tex}{B}_2 / 60^{\circ}{/tex} with the direction of {tex}B_1{/tex} .
solution: {tex}\overrightarrow{{dB}}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}(\overrightarrow{{d}} \times \overrightarrow{{r}})}{{r}^3}{/tex} {tex} {B}_1 =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex} {tex} {~B}_2 =\frac{\mu_0 \sqrt{3} {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex} {tex} {~B} =\sqrt{{B}_1^2+{B}_2^2} {/tex} {tex} \therefore {~B} =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} \sqrt{1+3} {/tex} {tex} B =\frac{\mu_0 I}{R} {/tex} {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{B_1}{B_2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} {/tex} Direction of net magnetic field is {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} with direction of {tex}{B}_2 / 60^{\circ}{/tex} with the direction of {tex}B_1{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. {tex}\overrightarrow{{dB}}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}(\overrightarrow{{d}} \times \overrightarrow{{r}})}{{r}^3}{/tex}
  2. {tex} {B}_1 =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex}
    {tex} {~B}_2 =\frac{\mu_0 \sqrt{3} {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex}
    {tex} {~B} =\sqrt{{B}_1^2+{B}_2^2} {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore {~B} =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} \sqrt{1+3} {/tex}
    {tex} B =\frac{\mu_0 I}{R} {/tex}
    {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{B_1}{B_2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} {/tex}
    Direction of net magnetic field is {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} with direction of {tex}{B}_2 / 60^{\circ}{/tex} with the direction of {tex}B_1{/tex}.

question_html: Two long, straight, parallel conductors carry steady currents in opposite directions. Explain the nature of the force of interaction between them. Obtain an expression for the magnitude of the force between the two conductors. Hence define one ampere.
question_text: Two long, straight, parallel conductors carry steady currents in opposite directions. Explain the nature of the force of interaction between them. Obtain an expression for the magnitude of the force between the two conductors. Hence define one ampere.
explanation: Nature of force is repulsive. Magnetic field due to current {tex}I_a{/tex} at all points of conductor {tex}b{/tex} {tex}B_{a b}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a}{2 \pi d} {/tex} directed downwards Force experienced by conductor b on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex} {tex}F_{a b}=I_b l B_{a b}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards left Similarly Force experienced by conductor a on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex} {tex}F_{b a}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards right One ampere is that steady current which when maintained in each of two very long straight parallel conductors of negligible cross- section, placed one metre apart in vacuum produces a force of {tex}2 \times 10^{-7} {~N} / {m}{/tex} on each conductor.
solution: Nature of force is repulsive. Magnetic field due to current {tex}I_a{/tex} at all points of conductor {tex}b{/tex} {tex}B_{a b}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a}{2 \pi d} {/tex} directed downwards Force experienced by conductor b on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex} {tex}F_{a b}=I_b l B_{a b}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards left Similarly Force experienced by conductor a on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex} {tex}F_{b a}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards right One ampere is that steady current which when maintained in each of two very long straight parallel conductors of negligible cross- section, placed one metre apart in vacuum produces a force of {tex}2 \times 10^{-7} {~N} / {m}{/tex} on each conductor.
solution_html:
Nature of force is repulsive.

Magnetic field due to current {tex}I_a{/tex} at all points of conductor {tex}b{/tex}
{tex}B_{a b}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a}{2 \pi d} {/tex} directed downwards
Force experienced by conductor b on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex}
{tex}F_{a b}=I_b l B_{a b}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards left
Similarly
Force experienced by conductor a on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex}
{tex}F_{b a}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards right
One ampere is that steady current which when maintained in each of two very long straight parallel conductors of negligible cross- section, placed one metre apart in vacuum produces a force of {tex}2 \times 10^{-7} {~N} / {m}{/tex} on each conductor.

explanation: Magnetic field due to inner circle at O, {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1}{4 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2} \times{/tex} 10 -5 T into the page Magnetic field due to semi-circle at O, {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r} \cdot \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{12 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{6} \times{/tex} 10 -5 T out of the page as magnetic fields are in opposite directions so B net = B 1 - B 2 {tex}=\left(\frac{\pi}{2} \times 10^{-5}-\frac{\pi}{6} \times 10^{-5}\right) T \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5} T{/tex} Direction of the net magnetic field is into the page, since B 1 > B 2 .
solution: Magnetic field due to inner circle at O, {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1}{4 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2} \times{/tex} 10 -5 T into the page Magnetic field due to semi-circle at O, {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r} \cdot \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{12 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{6} \times{/tex} 10 -5 T out of the page as magnetic fields are in opposite directions so B net = B 1 - B 2 {tex}=\left(\frac{\pi}{2} \times 10^{-5}-\frac{\pi}{6} \times 10^{-5}\right) T \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5} T{/tex} Direction of the net magnetic field is into the page, since B 1 > B 2 .

Magnetic field due to inner circle at O,
{tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1}{4 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2} \times{/tex} 10-5 T into the page
Magnetic field due to semi-circle at O,
{tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r} \cdot \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{12 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{6} \times{/tex} 10-5 T out of the page

as magnetic fields are in opposite directions so 
Bnet = B1 - B2
{tex}=\left(\frac{\pi}{2} \times 10^{-5}-\frac{\pi}{6} \times 10^{-5}\right) T \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5} T{/tex}
Direction of the net magnetic field is into the page, since B1 > B2.

subject: Physics
question_html: Describe the working principle of a moving coil galvanometer. Why is it necessary to use (i) a radial magnetic field and (ii) a cylindrical soft iron core in a galvanometer? Can a galvanometer as such be used for measuring the current? Explain.
question_text: Describe the working principle of a moving coil galvanometer. Why is it necessary to use (i) a radial magnetic field and (ii) a cylindrical soft iron core in a galvanometer? Can a galvanometer as such be used for measuring the current? Explain.
explanation: Principle of a moving coil galvanometer: A current-carrying coil placed in a magnetic field experiences a current dependent torque, which tends to rotate the coil and produces angular deflection. Radial magnetic field maximizes the torque on the coil and provides a linear current scale. A cylindrical soft iron core makes the field radial. This increases the strength of the magnetic field and hence increases the sensitivity of the galvanometer. A galvanometer as such cannot be used to measure current in a circuit due to two reasons: A galvanometer can detect only small currents and its coil is likely to be damaged by currents of (mA/A) range. For measuring currents, it has to be first calibrated.
solution: Principle of a moving coil galvanometer: A current-carrying coil placed in a magnetic field experiences a current dependent torque, which tends to rotate the coil and produces angular deflection. Radial magnetic field maximizes the torque on the coil and provides a linear current scale. A cylindrical soft iron core makes the field radial. This increases the strength of the magnetic field and hence increases the sensitivity of the galvanometer. A galvanometer as such cannot be used to measure current in a circuit due to two reasons: A galvanometer can detect only small currents and its coil is likely to be damaged by currents of (mA/A) range. For measuring currents, it has to be first calibrated.
solution_html:
Principle of a moving coil galvanometer: A current-carrying coil placed in a magnetic field experiences a current dependent torque, which tends to rotate the coil and produces angular deflection.

  1. Radial magnetic field maximizes the torque on the coil and provides a linear current scale.
  2. A cylindrical soft iron core makes the field radial. This increases the strength of the magnetic field and hence increases the sensitivity of the galvanometer.

A galvanometer as such cannot be used to measure current in a circuit due to two reasons:

  1. A galvanometer can detect only small currents and its coil is likely to be damaged by currents of (mA/A) range.
  2. For measuring currents, it has to be first calibrated.

question_html: A galvanometer of resistance 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} shows full scale deflection for a current of 4 mA. How will you convert it into a voltmeter to measure a voltage up to 3 V?
question_text: A galvanometer of resistance 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} shows full scale deflection for a current of 4 mA. How will you convert it into a voltmeter to measure a voltage up to 3 V?
explanation: R = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{Ig}}-\mathrm{G}{/tex} Here V = 3 V I g = 4 mA = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A G = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \times 10^{-3}}-16{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 1000}{4}-16{/tex} = 734 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence, in order to measure a voltage upto 3 V, a resistance of 734 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in series is added with the galvanometer.
solution: R = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{Ig}}-\mathrm{G}{/tex} Here V = 3 V I g = 4 mA = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A G = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \times 10^{-3}}-16{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 1000}{4}-16{/tex} = 734 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence, in order to measure a voltage upto 3 V, a resistance of 734 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in series is added with the galvanometer.
solution_html:
R = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{Ig}}-\mathrm{G}{/tex}
Here V = 3 V
Ig = 4 mA = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 A
G = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
R = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \times 10^{-3}}-16{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3 \times 1000}{4}-16{/tex}
= 734 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Hence, in order to measure a voltage upto 3 V, a resistance of  734 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in series is added with the galvanometer.

question_text: A proton and an electron travelling along parallel paths enter a region of the uniform magnetic field, acting perpendicular to their paths. Which of them will move in a circular path with a higher frequency?
explanation: Electron moves in a circular path with higher frequency.m e =mass of electron , m p =mass of proton. {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=q v B, r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} {tex}\omega=\frac{v}{r}=\frac{q B}{m}{/tex} {tex}\omega=2 \pi f \Rightarrow \frac{q B}{m}=2 \pi f \Rightarrow f \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex} Since m e < m p , therefore f e > f p Thus, the electron moves in circular path with higher frequency.

question_html: A square loop of side a carrying a current 12 is kept at distance x from an infinitely long straight wire carrying a current I1 as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the resultant force acting on the loop.
question_text: A square loop of side a carrying a current 12 is kept at distance x from an infinitely long straight wire carrying a current I 1 as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the resultant force acting on the loop.
explanation: Force per unit length between two parallel straight conductors {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I_1 I_2}{d}{/tex} Force on the part of the loop which is parallel to infinite straight wire and at a distance x from it. {tex}F_1=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I_1 I_2 a}{x}{/tex} (away from the infinite straight wire) Force on the part of the loop which is at a distance (x + a) from it, {tex}F_2=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{l_1 l_2 a}{(x+a)}{/tex} (towards the infinite straight wire) Net force, F = F 1 - F 2 {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} l_1 l_2a\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x+a}\right]{/tex} {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0 I_1 l_2 a^2}{2 \pi \;x(x+a)}{/tex} (away from the infinite straight wire)
solution: Force per unit length between two parallel straight conductors {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I_1 I_2}{d}{/tex} Force on the part of the loop which is parallel to infinite straight wire and at a distance x from it. {tex}F_1=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I_1 I_2 a}{x}{/tex} (away from the infinite straight wire) Force on the part of the loop which is at a distance (x + a) from it, {tex}F_2=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{l_1 l_2 a}{(x+a)}{/tex} (towards the infinite straight wire) Net force, F = F 1 - F 2 {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} l_1 l_2a\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x+a}\right]{/tex} {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0 I_1 l_2 a^2}{2 \pi \;x(x+a)}{/tex} (away from the infinite straight wire)
solution_html:
Force per unit length between two parallel straight conductors 
{tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I_1 I_2}{d}{/tex}
Force on the part of the loop which is parallel to infinite straight wire and at a distance x from it.
{tex}F_1=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I_1 I_2 a}{x}{/tex}
(away from the infinite straight wire) Force on the part of the loop which is at a distance (x + a) from it,
{tex}F_2=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{l_1 l_2 a}{(x+a)}{/tex}
(towards the infinite straight wire)
Net force, F = F1 - F2
{tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} l_1 l_2a\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x+a}\right]{/tex}
{tex}F=\frac{\mu_0 I_1 l_2 a^2}{2 \pi \;x(x+a)}{/tex}
(away from the infinite straight wire)

Describe the working principle of a moving coil galvanometer. Why is it necessary to use

  1. a radial magnetic field and
  2. a cylindrical soft iron core in a galvanometer?

Write the expression for current sensitivity of the galvanometer. Can a galvanometer as such be used for measuring the current? Explain.
question_text: Describe the working principle of a moving coil galvanometer. Why is it necessary to use a radial magnetic field and a cylindrical soft iron core in a galvanometer? Write the expression for current sensitivity of the galvanometer. Can a galvanometer as such be used for measuring the current? Explain.
explanation: A current-carrying coil, when placed in an external magnetic field, experiences magnetic torque. The angle through which the coil is deflected due to the effect of the magnetic torque is proportional to the magnitude of current in the coil. The radial magnetic field is used in a suspended coil galvanometer to provide a uniform torque on the coil because it provides a uniform magnetic field. It ensures that the magnetic field is radial and uniform throughout the area of the coil. In order to produce magnetic field inside the galvanometer device, we can use any magnetic material but soft iron core is especially used in galvanometer because Soft iron is ferromagnetic in nature which in turn strengthen the magnetic field lines, and stronger the magnetic field lines passing through the coil larger Expression for current sensitivity {tex}I_{s}=\frac{\theta}{I} {/tex} The galvanometer is a very sensitive device. For a small amount of current, the galvanometer deflection will be maximum. Hence, it may get damaged when a strong current is passed through the galvanometer. So the galvanometer is not used to measure the current.
solution: A current-carrying coil, when placed in an external magnetic field, experiences magnetic torque. The angle through which the coil is deflected due to the effect of the magnetic torque is proportional to the magnitude of current in the coil. The radial magnetic field is used in a suspended coil galvanometer to provide a uniform torque on the coil because it provides a uniform magnetic field. It ensures that the magnetic field is radial and uniform throughout the area of the coil. In order to produce magnetic field inside the galvanometer device, we can use any magnetic material but soft iron core is especially used in galvanometer because Soft iron is ferromagnetic in nature which in turn strengthen the magnetic field lines, and stronger the magnetic field lines passing through the coil larger Expression for current sensitivity {tex}I_{s}=\frac{\theta}{I} {/tex} The galvanometer is a very sensitive device. For a small amount of current, the galvanometer deflection will be maximum. Hence, it may get damaged when a strong current is passed through the galvanometer. So the galvanometer is not used to measure the current.
solution_html:
A current-carrying coil, when placed in an external magnetic field, experiences magnetic torque. The angle through which the coil is deflected due to the effect of the magnetic torque is proportional to the magnitude of current in the coil.

  1. The radial magnetic field is used in a suspended coil galvanometer to provide a uniform torque on the coil because it provides a uniform magnetic field. It ensures that the magnetic field is radial and uniform throughout the area of the coil.
  2. In order to produce magnetic field inside the galvanometer device, we can use any magnetic material but soft iron core is especially used in galvanometer because Soft iron is ferromagnetic in nature which in turn strengthen the magnetic field lines, and stronger the magnetic field lines passing through the coil larger

Expression for current sensitivity

{tex}I_{s}=\frac{\theta}{I} {/tex}

The galvanometer is a very sensitive device. For a small amount of current, the galvanometer deflection will be maximum. Hence, it may get damaged when a strong current is passed through the galvanometer. So the galvanometer is not used to measure the current.

question_html: Derive the expression for the torque {tex}\tau{/tex} acting on a rectangular current loop of area A placed in a uniform magnetic field B. Show that {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex} where {tex}\vec m{/tex} is the magnetic moment of the current loop given by {tex}\vec{m}=I\vec A{/tex}
question_text: Derive the expression for the torque {tex}\tau{/tex} acting on a rectangular current loop of area A placed in a uniform magnetic field B. Show that {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex} where {tex}\vec m{/tex} is the magnetic moment of the current loop given by {tex}\vec{m}=I\vec A{/tex}
explanation: The force, on a wire of length l, carrying a current {tex}I{/tex} , in a magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} , is given by {tex}\vec F{/tex} = {tex}IlB{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\text { I } \vec{l} \times \vec{B}{/tex} For a rectangular loop, placed as shown, in a magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} , |Force on arm BC| = |Force on arm DA| = {tex}IlB{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} where, {tex}\theta{/tex} = angle between side BC and {tex}\vec B{/tex} These two forces add up to zero as they are collinear (along the axis of the coil) and act in opposite directions. |Force on arm AB| = |Force on arm CD| = IbB These two equal and opposite forces are not collinear. The perpendicular distance between their lines of action is, as shown, {tex}2 \times \frac{a}{2}{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque acting on the coil, has a magnitude {tex}\tau{/tex} , where, {tex}\tau{/tex} = (lbB) {tex}\times{/tex} (a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = IABsin {tex}\theta{/tex} (A = ab = area of the coil) In vector form, {tex}\vec{\tau} =I \vec{A} \times \vec{B} {/tex} But {tex}\overrightarrow{I A} =\vec{m},{/tex} as given {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex}
solution: The force, on a wire of length l, carrying a current {tex}I{/tex} , in a magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} , is given by {tex}\vec F{/tex} = {tex}IlB{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\text { I } \vec{l} \times \vec{B}{/tex} For a rectangular loop, placed as shown, in a magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} , |Force on arm BC| = |Force on arm DA| = {tex}IlB{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} where, {tex}\theta{/tex} = angle between side BC and {tex}\vec B{/tex} These two forces add up to zero as they are collinear (along the axis of the coil) and act in opposite directions. |Force on arm AB| = |Force on arm CD| = IbB These two equal and opposite forces are not collinear. The perpendicular distance between their lines of action is, as shown, {tex}2 \times \frac{a}{2}{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque acting on the coil, has a magnitude {tex}\tau{/tex} , where, {tex}\tau{/tex} = (lbB) {tex}\times{/tex} (a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = IABsin {tex}\theta{/tex} (A = ab = area of the coil) In vector form, {tex}\vec{\tau} =I \vec{A} \times \vec{B} {/tex} But {tex}\overrightarrow{I A} =\vec{m},{/tex} as given {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex}
solution_html:
The force, on a wire of length l, carrying a current {tex}I{/tex}, in a magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex}, is given by {tex}\vec F{/tex} = {tex}IlB{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
= {tex}\text { I } \vec{l} \times \vec{B}{/tex}
For a rectangular loop, placed as shown, in a magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex},

|Force on arm BC| = |Force on arm DA| = {tex}IlB{/tex}sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
where, {tex}\theta{/tex} = angle between side BC and {tex}\vec B{/tex}
These two forces add up to zero as they are collinear (along the axis of the coil) and act in opposite directions.
|Force on arm AB| = |Force on arm CD| = IbB
These two equal and opposite forces are not collinear. The perpendicular distance between their lines of action is, as shown, {tex}2 \times \frac{a}{2}{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque acting on the coil, has a magnitude {tex}\tau{/tex},
where,
{tex}\tau{/tex} = (lbB) {tex}\times{/tex} (a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}) = IABsin {tex}\theta{/tex} (A = ab = area of the coil)
In vector form,
{tex}\vec{\tau} =I \vec{A} \times \vec{B} {/tex}
But {tex}\overrightarrow{I A} =\vec{m},{/tex} as given
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex}

solution: A galvanometer is converted into an ammeter when a suitable shunt resistance is connected in parallel with the galvanometer. Glg = (I - Ig)S {tex}\mathrm{S}=\left(\frac{1 g}{l-1 g}\right) \mathrm{G}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{R}_{eff}=\frac{G S}{G+S}{/tex}
solution_html:
A galvanometer is converted into an ammeter when a suitable shunt resistance is connected in parallel with the galvanometer.

Glg = (I - Ig)S
{tex}\mathrm{S}=\left(\frac{1 g}{l-1 g}\right) \mathrm{G}{/tex}

The loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} and is free to rotate about an axis which is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} . Prove that the loop experiences no net force, but a torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} .

1. {tex}\overrightarrow{{m}}={I} \overrightarrow{{A}}{/tex}
  2. {tex} {F}_1={F}_2={IbB} {/tex}
    {tex}{F}_1=\text { Force on } {AB} \text { into the plane } {/tex}
    {tex} {F}_2=\text { Force on } {CD} \text { out of the plane } {/tex}

    Since forces are equal & opposite so net force {tex}=0{/tex}
    Both forces form a couple, magnitude of torque acting on the coil is

question_html: Deduce the expression for the torque experienced by a rectangular loop carrying a steady current I and placed in a uniform magnetic field B. Indicate the direction of the torque acting on the loop.
question_text: Deduce the expression for the torque experienced by a rectangular loop carrying a steady current I and placed in a uniform magnetic field B. Indicate the direction of the torque acting on the loop.
explanation: Let a current carrying rectangular loop PQRS is carrying a steady current I placed in a uniform magnetic field B keeping the axis of the coil perpendicular to the field as shown in figure. Let at any instant the area vector A makes an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with the direction of magnetic field intensity B. Let length and breadth of the coil are I and b respectively. Now, magnetic force on PS arm of the coil is given by {tex}F _ { 1 } = BIl \sin 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} PS || axis of coil, {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} ] F 1 = BIl ...... (i) By Fleming's left hand rule, the direction of the above force is perpendicular to SP and B is along upward direction. Similarly, force on QR arm of the coil F 2 = BIl sin 90° ..... (ii) The direction of force is perpendicular to QR and B is along downward direction {tex}\because{/tex} F 1 and F 2 are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, parallel to each other acting on the loop forms a couple which try to rotate the coil. Now, force on RS part of the coil {tex}F _ { 3 } = I B b \sin \left( 90 ^ { \circ } + \theta \right) \Rightarrow F _ { 3 } = I B b \cos \theta{/tex} and also force on PQ part of the coil {tex}F _ { 4 } = I B b \sin \left( 90 ^ { \circ } - \theta \right) = I B b \cos \theta{/tex} But by Fleming's left hand rule, F 3 and F 4 are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction along the same line of action. Therefore, they balance each other. Now, torque due to F 1 and F 2 is given by {tex}\tau {/tex} = force {tex} \times {/tex} perpendicular distance between Iines of action of F 1 and F 2 . {tex}\tau = F \times b \sin \theta{/tex} But, {tex}F _ { 1 } = F _ { 2 } = F = I B l, \therefore \tau = ( I B l ) \times ( b \sin \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tau = I B ( l b ) \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tau = I B A \sin \theta{/tex} where, A = lb = area of coil. For N turns of the coil, we have {tex}\tau = N I A B \sin \theta{/tex} , this is the required expression of the torque in the current carrying rectangular loop.

F1 = BIl ...... (i)

The direction of force is perpendicular to QR and B is along downward direction
{tex}\because{/tex} F1 and F2 are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, parallel to each other acting on the loop forms a couple which try to rotate the coil.

{tex}F _ { 3 } = I B b \sin \left( 90 ^ { \circ } + \theta \right) \Rightarrow F _ { 3 } = I B b \cos \theta{/tex}
and also force on PQ part of the coil
{tex}F _ { 4 } = I B b \sin \left( 90 ^ { \circ } - \theta \right) = I B b \cos \theta{/tex}
But by Fleming's left hand rule, F3 and F4 are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction along the same line of action. Therefore, they balance each other.
Now, torque due to F1 and F2 is given by
{tex}\tau {/tex} = force {tex} \times {/tex} perpendicular distance between Iines of action of F1 and F2.
{tex}\tau = F \times b \sin \theta{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \tau = I B ( l b ) \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \tau = I B A \sin \theta{/tex}
where, A = lb = area of coil.
For N turns of the coil, we have

question_html:
Consider the motion of a charged particle of mass m and charge q moving with velocity v in a magnetic field B.

  1. If v is perpendicular to B, then show that it describes a circular path having angular frequency {tex}\omega = \frac {q B }{ m}{/tex}.
  2. If the velocity v has a component parallel to the magnetic field B, then trace the path described by the particle. Justify your answer.
question_text: Consider the motion of a charged particle of mass m and charge q moving with velocity v in a magnetic field B. If v is perpendicular to B, then show that it describes a circular path having angular frequency {tex}\omega = \frac {q B }{ m}{/tex} . If the velocity v has a component parallel to the magnetic field B, then trace the path described by the particle. Justify your answer.
explanation: Force acting on the charged particle, moving with a velocity v, in magnetic field B. {tex}\vec F = q(\vec v× \vec B){/tex} As, {tex}\mathbf { v } \perp \mathbf { B } , | \text { Force } | = q v B{/tex} Since, {tex}\mathbf { F } \perp \mathbf { v }{/tex} , it acts as a centripetal force and makes the particle move in a circular path, in the plane, perpendicular to the magnetic field. Now, {tex}q v B = \frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r }{/tex} (as the magnetic force supplies the required centripetal force) {tex}\therefore \quad r = \frac { m v } { q B }{/tex} Now, {tex}\omega = \frac { v } { r }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \omega = \frac { q B } { m }{/tex} Component of velocity, v parallel to magnetic field, will make the particle move along the field. Perpendicular component of velocity, v, will cause the particle to move along circular path in the plane perpendicular to the magnetic field. That means the particle will revolve in a circular path and will shift in the forward direction as shown in the figure above. Hence, the particle will travel the helix path.
solution: Force acting on the charged particle, moving with a velocity v, in magnetic field B. {tex}\vec F = q(\vec v× \vec B){/tex} As, {tex}\mathbf { v } \perp \mathbf { B } , | \text { Force } | = q v B{/tex} Since, {tex}\mathbf { F } \perp \mathbf { v }{/tex} , it acts as a centripetal force and makes the particle move in a circular path, in the plane, perpendicular to the magnetic field. Now, {tex}q v B = \frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r }{/tex} (as the magnetic force supplies the required centripetal force) {tex}\therefore \quad r = \frac { m v } { q B }{/tex} Now, {tex}\omega = \frac { v } { r }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \omega = \frac { q B } { m }{/tex} Component of velocity, v parallel to magnetic field, will make the particle move along the field. Perpendicular component of velocity, v, will cause the particle to move along circular path in the plane perpendicular to the magnetic field. That means the particle will revolve in a circular path and will shift in the forward direction as shown in the figure above. Hence, the particle will travel the helix path.
solution_html:
1. Force acting on the charged particle, moving with a velocity v, in magnetic field B.
    {tex}\vec F = q(\vec v× \vec B){/tex}
    As, {tex}\mathbf { v } \perp \mathbf { B } , | \text { Force } | = q v B{/tex}
    Since, {tex}\mathbf { F } \perp \mathbf { v }{/tex}, it acts as a centripetal force and makes the particle move in a circular path, in the plane, perpendicular to the magnetic field.
    Now, {tex}q v B = \frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r }{/tex}(as the magnetic force supplies the required centripetal force)
    {tex}\therefore \quad r = \frac { m v } { q B }{/tex}
    Now, {tex}\omega = \frac { v } { r }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \omega = \frac { q B } { m }{/tex}

  2. Component of velocity, v parallel to magnetic field, will make the particle move along the field.
    Perpendicular component of velocity, v, will cause the particle to move along circular path in the plane perpendicular to the magnetic field. That means the particle will revolve in a circular path and will shift in the forward direction as shown in the figure above. Hence, the particle will travel the helix path.

question_html: A proton, a deutron and an alpha particle having the same kinetic energy are allowed to pass through a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to their direction of motion. Compare the radii o f their circular paths.
question_text: A proton, a deutron and an alpha particle having the same kinetic energy are allowed to pass through a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to their direction of motion. Compare the radii o f their circular paths.
explanation: If m p , m d and m a are the masses of proton, deutron and a-particle respectively, then m d = 2m p and m a = 4m p Also q d = q p and {tex}q_{\alpha}{/tex} = 2q p Kinetic energy, {tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Velocity {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}{/tex} Radius of the circular path of a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad r_{p}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K}}{q_{p} B}, r_{d}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{d} K}}{q_{d} B}{/tex} and {tex}r_{\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} K}}{q_{\alpha} B}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{d}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{d}}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{2 m_{p}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}}=\frac{2 q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}}=1{/tex} Hence {tex}r_{p}: r_{d}: r_{\alpha}=1: \sqrt{2}: \mathbf{1}{/tex}
solution: If m p , m d and m a are the masses of proton, deutron and a-particle respectively, then m d = 2m p and m a = 4m p Also q d = q p and {tex}q_{\alpha}{/tex} = 2q p Kinetic energy, {tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Velocity {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}{/tex} Radius of the circular path of a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad r_{p}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K}}{q_{p} B}, r_{d}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{d} K}}{q_{d} B}{/tex} and {tex}r_{\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} K}}{q_{\alpha} B}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{d}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{d}}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{2 m_{p}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}}=\frac{2 q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}}=1{/tex} Hence {tex}r_{p}: r_{d}: r_{\alpha}=1: \sqrt{2}: \mathbf{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
If mp , md and ma are the masses of proton, deutron and a-particle respectively, then
md = 2mp and ma = 4mp
Also qd = qp and {tex}q_{\alpha}{/tex} = 2qp
Kinetic energy,
{tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Velocity {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}{/tex}
Radius of the circular path of a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by
{tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad r_{p}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K}}{q_{p} B}, r_{d}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{d} K}}{q_{d} B}{/tex} and {tex}r_{\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} K}}{q_{\alpha} B}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{d}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{d}}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{2 m_{p}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}}=\frac{2 q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}}=1{/tex}
Hence {tex}r_{p}: r_{d}: r_{\alpha}=1: \sqrt{2}: \mathbf{1}{/tex}

question_html: A long wire is bent into a circular coil of one turn and then into a circular coil of a smaller radius having n turns. If the same current passes in both cases find the ratio of the magnetic fields produced at the centres in the two cases.
question_text: A long wire is bent into a circular coil of one turn and then into a circular coil of a smaller radius having n turns. If the same current passes in both cases find the ratio of the magnetic fields produced at the centres in the two cases.
explanation: Let l be the length of the wire. When the wire is first bent in the form of one turn circular coil, {tex}l=2 \pi r_{1}{/tex} or {tex}r_{1}=\frac{l}{2 \pi}, N=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 r_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \times 1 \times I}{2 \times(l / 2 \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi l}{l}{/tex} When the wire is bent in the form of n-turn coil, {tex}l=n \times 2 \pi r_{2} \text { or } r_{2}=\frac{l}{2 n \pi}, N=n{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} n I}{2(l / 2 n \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi n^{2} I}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\frac{1}{n^{2}}=1: n^{2}{/tex}
solution: Let l be the length of the wire. When the wire is first bent in the form of one turn circular coil, {tex}l=2 \pi r_{1}{/tex} or {tex}r_{1}=\frac{l}{2 \pi}, N=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 r_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \times 1 \times I}{2 \times(l / 2 \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi l}{l}{/tex} When the wire is bent in the form of n-turn coil, {tex}l=n \times 2 \pi r_{2} \text { or } r_{2}=\frac{l}{2 n \pi}, N=n{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} n I}{2(l / 2 n \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi n^{2} I}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\frac{1}{n^{2}}=1: n^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let l be the length of the wire. When the wire is first bent in the form of one turn circular coil, {tex}l=2 \pi r_{1}{/tex} or {tex}r_{1}=\frac{l}{2 \pi}, N=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 r_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \times 1 \times I}{2 \times(l / 2 \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi l}{l}{/tex}
When the wire is bent in the form of n-turn coil,
{tex}l=n \times 2 \pi r_{2} \text { or } r_{2}=\frac{l}{2 n \pi}, N=n{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} n I}{2(l / 2 n \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi n^{2} I}{l}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\frac{1}{n^{2}}=1: n^{2}{/tex}

question_html: Define current sensitivity and voltage sensitivity of a galvanometer. Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity of galvanometer. Justify.
question_text: Define current sensitivity and voltage sensitivity of a galvanometer. Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity of galvanometer. Justify.
explanation: Current sensitivity: It is defined as the deflection of coil per unit current flowing on it. Current Sensitivity, {tex}S_{\theta}=\frac{\theta}{I}=\frac{N A B}{C}{/tex} Voltage sensitivity: It is defined on the deflection of coil per unit potential difference across its ends. Voltage Sensitivity, {tex}S_{V}=\frac{\theta}{V}=\frac{N A B}{G C}{/tex} Where G is resistance of galvanometer. Justification: When number of turns N is doulbed, then the current sensitivity {tex}(\propto N){/tex} is doubled; but at the same time, the resistance of galvanometer coil (G) will also be doubled, So voltage sensitivity {tex} \left(S_V \propto \frac{N}{G}\right){/tex} will remain unchanged; hence increasing current sensitivity does not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity.

Current Sensitivity, {tex}S_{\theta}=\frac{\theta}{I}=\frac{N A B}{C}{/tex} Voltage sensitivity: It is defined on the deflection of coil per unit potential difference across its ends. Voltage Sensitivity, {tex}S_{V}=\frac{\theta}{V}=\frac{N A B}{G C}{/tex} Where G is resistance of galvanometer. Justification: When number of turns N is doulbed, then the current sensitivity {tex}(\propto N){/tex} is doubled; but at the same time, the resistance of galvanometer coil (G) will also be doubled, So voltage sensitivity {tex} \left(S_V \propto \frac{N}{G}\right){/tex} will remain unchanged; hence increasing current sensitivity does not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity.
solution_html:
Current sensitivity: It is defined as the deflection of coil per unit current flowing on it.
Current Sensitivity,
{tex}S_{\theta}=\frac{\theta}{I}=\frac{N A B}{C}{/tex}

Voltage sensitivity: It is defined on the deflection of coil per unit potential difference across its ends.
Voltage Sensitivity,
{tex}S_{V}=\frac{\theta}{V}=\frac{N A B}{G C}{/tex}
Where G is resistance of galvanometer.

Justification: When number of turns N is doulbed, then the current sensitivity {tex}(\propto N){/tex} is doubled; but at the same time, the resistance of galvanometer coil (G) will also be doubled, So voltage sensitivity {tex} \left(S_V \propto \frac{N}{G}\right){/tex} will remain unchanged; hence increasing current sensitivity does not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity.

question_html:
The magnitude F of the force between two straight parallel current-carrying conductors kept at a distance d apart in the air is given by F = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{I_{1} I_{2}}{d}{/tex} where I1 and I2 are the currents flowing through the two wires
Use this expression, and the sign convention that the: " Force of attraction is assigned a negative sign and force of repulsion assigned a positive sign". Draw graphs showing the dependence of F on:

  1. I1I2 when d kept constant
  2. when the product I1I2 is maintained at a constant positive value.
  3. when the product I1I2 is maintained at a constant negative value.
question_text: The magnitude F of the force between two straight parallel current-carrying conductors kept at a distance d apart in the air is given by F = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{I_{1} I_{2}}{d}{/tex} where I 1 and I 2 are the currents flowing through the two wires Use this expression, and the sign convention that the: " Force of attraction is assigned a negative sign and force of repulsion assigned a positive sign". Draw graphs showing the dependence of F on: I 1 I 2 when d kept constant when the product I 1 I 2 is maintained at a constant positive value. when the product I 1 I 2 is maintained at a constant negative value.
explanation: The magnitude F of the force between two straight parallel current-carrying conductors kept at a distance d apart in the air We know that F is an attractive (- ve) force when the currents l 1 and I 2 are 'like' currents, i.e., when the product l 1 l 2 is positive or when they are flowing in the same direction. Similarly, F is a repulsive (+ve) force when the currents l 1 and I 2 are 'unlike' currents, i.e., when the product l 1 l 2 is negative or when they are flowing in the opposite direction. Now F {tex}\propto{/tex} (l 1 l 2 ), when d is kept constant and F {tex}\propto \frac{1}{d}{/tex} when l 1 l 2 is kept constant. The required graphs, therefore, have the forms shown below.

    {tex}\vec{d B}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} I \frac{\vec{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3}{/tex}

1. With the help of a diagram, explain the principle and working of a moving coil galvanometer.
  2. What is the importance of a radial magnetic field and how is it produced?
  3. Why is it necessary to introduce a cylindrical soft iron core inside the coil of a galvanometer?
  4. Increasing the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may not necessarily increase its voltage sensitivity. Justify this statement.
question_text: With the help of a diagram, explain the principle and working of a moving coil galvanometer. What is the importance of a radial magnetic field and how is it produced? Why is it necessary to introduce a cylindrical soft iron core inside the coil of a galvanometer? Increasing the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may not necessarily increase its voltage sensitivity. Justify this statement.
explanation: Principle and working: When current (I) is passed in the coil, torque τ acts on the coil, given by {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIABsin {tex}\theta{/tex} When the magnetic field is radial, as in the case of cylindrical pole pieces and soft iron core, then in every position of coil the plane of the coil, is parallel to the magnetic field lines, so that {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o and sin 90 o = 1 Deflecting torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB If C is the torsional rigidity of the wire and {tex}\theta{/tex} is the twist of suspension strip, then restoring torque = C {tex}\theta{/tex} For equilibrium, deflecting torque = restoring torque i.e. NIAB = C {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N A B}{C}{/tex} I ...(i) i.e. {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\propto{/tex} I Importance (or function) of uniform radial magnetic field: In radial magnetic field sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1, so torque is {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB. This makes the deflection ( {tex}\theta{/tex} ) proportional to current.To produce radial magnetic field pole pieces of permanent magnet are made cylindrical and a soft iron core is placed between them. The soft iron core helps in making the field radial and reduce energy losses produced due to eddy currents. lection of coil is directly proportional to current flowing in the coil and hence we can construct a linear scale. Importance of uniform radial magnetic field: Torque for current carrying coil in a magnetic field is {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} In radial magnetic field Sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1, so torque is {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} This makes the deflection proportional to current. In other words, the radial magnetic field makes the scale linear. The cylindrical soft iron core makes the filed radial and increases the strength of the magnetic field. i.e. the magnitude of the torque. A voltmeter is used to measure p.d. across a resistance in an electrical circuit. If a voltmeter has very high resistance, it will not affect the resistance of circuit, hence reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer on a voltmeter, a high resistance in series is required. An ammeter is used to measure current in circuit, hence it is connected in series with the circuit. If an ammeter has very low resistance it will not affect the circuit - resistance and so reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer as an ammeter, a shunt (small resistance in parallel) is used.

question_text: State Biot-Savart law expressing it in vector form. Use it to obtain the magnetic field, at an axial point, distant r from the centre of a circular coil of radius a carrying a current I. Hence, compare the magnitudes of the magnetic field of this coil at the centre and at an axial point for which {tex}r=\sqrt{3} a{/tex}
explanation: Biot- Savart law: The magnetic field due to a current element of length dl carrying a current I at a point P, which is at a distance r from it is directly proportional to the current I and element length {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} . Inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the current element and the point P. The direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane containing {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} Vector form: {tex}\overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3}{/tex} Due to the current element dl considered at A the magnetic field at P is- dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{|\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}|}{r^3}{/tex} Since, dl {tex}\times{/tex} r = dlr dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{r^2}{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} Consider a current element opposite to A, that is at B, then we can see that the y components of the magnetic, field due to this current element cancel and X component is only present. So we can say that the net magnetic field is along the X-direction. dB X = dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Net magnetic field at P is B = {tex}\int{/tex} dB X = {tex}\int{/tex} dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)} {/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} dl = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R 2 \pi R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}

Inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the current element and the point P. The direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane containing {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} Vector form: {tex}\overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3}{/tex} Due to the current element dl considered at A the magnetic field at P is- dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{|\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}|}{r^3}{/tex} Since, dl {tex}\times{/tex} r = dlr dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{r^2}{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} Consider a current element opposite to A, that is at B, then we can see that the y components of the magnetic, field due to this current element cancel and X component is only present. So we can say that the net magnetic field is along the X-direction. dB X = dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Net magnetic field at P is B = {tex}\int{/tex} dB X = {tex}\int{/tex} dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)} {/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} dl = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R 2 \pi R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Biot- Savart law:
The magnetic field due to a current element of length dl carrying a current I at a point P, which is at a distance r from it is

  1. directly proportional to the current I and element length {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex}.
  2. Inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the current element and the point P.

The direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane containing {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}{/tex}
Vector form:
{tex}\overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3}{/tex}

Due to the current element dl considered at A the magnetic field at P is-
dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{|\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}|}{r^3}{/tex}
Since, dl {tex}\times{/tex} r = dlr
dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{r^2}{/tex}
dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex}
Consider a current element opposite to A, that is at B, then we can see that the y components of the magnetic, field due to this current element cancel and X component is only present.
So we can say that the net magnetic field is along the X-direction.
dBX = dB cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
Net magnetic field at P is
B = {tex}\int{/tex}dBX = {tex}\int{/tex}dB cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
= {tex}\int{/tex}{tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex}cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
= {tex}\int{/tex}{tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)} {/tex}{tex}\frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex}dl = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R 2 \pi R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}

1. What is current sensitivity of a galvanometer? Show how the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may be increased. "Increasing the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may not necessarily increase its voltage sensitivity." Explain.
  2. A moving coil galvanometer has a resistance {tex}15 \Omega{/tex} and takes 20 mA to produce full scale deflection. How can this galvanometer be converted into a voltmeter of range 0 to {tex}100 {~V} ?{/tex}
question_text: What is current sensitivity of a galvanometer? Show how the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may be increased. "Increasing the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may not necessarily increase its voltage sensitivity." Explain. A moving coil galvanometer has a resistance {tex}15 \Omega{/tex} and takes 20 mA to produce full scale deflection. How can this galvanometer be converted into a voltmeter of range 0 to {tex}100 {~V} ?{/tex}
explanation: Deflection produced per unit current is called its current sensitivity. Current sensitivity can be increased by (a) increasing number of turns in coil (b) increasing area of coil in magnetic field (c) decreasing K (Torsional Constant) (any one) {tex}V_s=\frac{\theta}{V}=\frac{N B A}{K R}{/tex} If current sensitivity is increased by increasing number of turns of the coil, the resistance of the galvanometer will also increase. Thus voltage sensitivity may not increase. {tex} V =I_G(R+G) {/tex} {tex} R =\frac{V}{I_G}-G {/tex} {tex} =\frac{100}{20 \times 10^{-3}}-15 {/tex} {tex} =5000-15 {/tex} {tex} =4985 \Omega {/tex} By connecting {tex}4985 \Omega{/tex} in series with galvanometer it is converted to voltmeter of range {tex}(0-100 {~V}){/tex}

Thus voltage sensitivity may not increase. {tex} V =I_G(R+G) {/tex} {tex} R =\frac{V}{I_G}-G {/tex} {tex} =\frac{100}{20 \times 10^{-3}}-15 {/tex} {tex} =5000-15 {/tex} {tex} =4985 \Omega {/tex} By connecting {tex}4985 \Omega{/tex} in series with galvanometer it is converted to voltmeter of range {tex}(0-100 {~V}){/tex}

    Current sensitivity can be increased by
    (a) increasing number of turns in coil
    (b) increasing area of coil in magnetic field
    (c) decreasing K (Torsional Constant)
    (any one)
    {tex}V_s=\frac{\theta}{V}=\frac{N B A}{K R}{/tex}
    If current sensitivity is increased by increasing number of turns of the coil, the resistance of the galvanometer will also increase. Thus voltage sensitivity may not increase.
  2. {tex} V =I_G(R+G) {/tex}
    {tex} R =\frac{V}{I_G}-G {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{100}{20 \times 10^{-3}}-15 {/tex}
    {tex} =5000-15 {/tex}
    {tex} =4985 \Omega {/tex}
    By connecting {tex}4985 \Omega{/tex} in series with galvanometer it is converted to voltmeter of range {tex}(0-100 {~V}){/tex}

1. Write the principle and explain the working of a moving coil galvanometer. A galvanometer as such cannot be used to measure the current in a circuit. Why?
  2. Why is the magnetic field made radial in a moving coil galvanometer? How is it achieved?
question_text: Write the principle and explain the working of a moving coil galvanometer. A galvanometer as such cannot be used to measure the current in a circuit. Why? Why is the magnetic field made radial in a moving coil galvanometer? How is it achieved?
explanation: Principle- When a current carrying coil is placed in a uniform magnetic field, it experiences a torque. Working:- When a current flows through the coil of a galvanometer, a torque acts on it. It is given as {tex}\tau=N i A B \sin \theta{/tex} For radial magnetic field; sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 The spring provides a counter or restoring toqrue {tex}k \phi{/tex} . {tex}k \phi{/tex} = NiAB In equilibrium; {tex}\phi=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) i{/tex} Galvanometer cannot be used as such to measure current because: It has large resistance and hence will decrease the value of current in the circuit. The magnetic field is made radial in a moving coil galvanometer so that the area vector of the coil is always normal to the magnetic field {tex}(\vec{B}){/tex} Hence,sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 always The magnetic field is made radial in a moving coil galvanometer to make the scale linear. It is achieved by using curved magnetic poles.

1. With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the principle of working of a moving coil galvanometer. Write the purpose of using (I) radial magnetic field, and (II) soft iron core, in it.
  2. Define current sensitivity of a galvanometer. "Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity." Give reason.
question_text: With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the principle of working of a moving coil galvanometer. Write the purpose of using (I) radial magnetic field, and (II) soft iron core, in it. Define current sensitivity of a galvanometer. "Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity." Give reason.
explanation: Principle: A current carrying coil placed in uniform magnetic field, experiences a torque. Radial magnetic field makes the scale linear Use of soft iron core is to increase the strength of magnetic field/ increase sensitivity of the galvanometer. Current sensitivity is defined as deflection per unit current. Voltage sensitivity {tex} V_s=\frac{\Phi}{V}=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) \frac{{I}}{V}=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) \frac{1}{R}{/tex} Increase in number of turns, increases the current sensitivity and resistance of the galvanometer in the same proportion of current sensitivity therefore Voltage sensitivity remains unchanged.

Voltage sensitivity {tex} V_s=\frac{\Phi}{V}=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) \frac{{I}}{V}=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) \frac{1}{R}{/tex} Increase in number of turns, increases the current sensitivity and resistance of the galvanometer in the same proportion of current sensitivity therefore Voltage sensitivity remains unchanged.

1. Principle:A current carrying coil placed in uniform magnetic field, experiences a torque.
    1. Radial magnetic field makes the scale linear
    2. Use of soft iron core is to increase the strength of magnetic field/ increase sensitivity of the galvanometer.
  2. Current sensitivity is defined as deflection per unit current.

question_html:
1. Using Ampere's circuital law, obtain the expression for the magnetic field due to a long solenoid at a point inside the solenoid on its axis.
  2. In what respect is a toroid different from a solenoid? Draw and compare the pattern of the magnetic field lines in two cases.
  3. How is the magnetic field inside a given solenoid made strong?
question_text: Using Ampere's circuital law, obtain the expression for the magnetic field due to a long solenoid at a point inside the solenoid on its axis. In what respect is a toroid different from a solenoid? Draw and compare the pattern of the magnetic field lines in two cases. How is the magnetic field inside a given solenoid made strong?
explanation: Consider a symmetrical long solenoid having a number of turns per unit length equal to n. Let I be the current flowing in the solenoid, then by the right-hand rule, the magnetic field is parallel to the axis of the solenoid. Field inside the solenoid: Consider a closed path abcd. Now, using Ampere's circuital law to this path, we have {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu \times 0{/tex} Therefore, B = 0 This implies, the magnetic field outside the solenoid is 0. Field inside the solenoid: Consider a closed path pqrs. The line integral of the magnetic field is given by, {tex}\oint_{\text {pqrs }} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{s p} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} ....(i) For path pq, {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} are along the same direction, {tex}\therefore \quad \int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{S p} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot d \vec{l}=\int B d l \cos 90^{\circ}{/tex} = 0 For path rs, B = 0 because outside the solenoid field is zero. {tex}\therefore \quad \int_{r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex} Now, using Ampere's law, Using these equations, equation (i) gives, {tex}\oint_{p q r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=B l{/tex} Now, using Ampere's law, {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d} I=\mu_{0} I{/tex} This implies, {tex}B l=\mu_{0}(n l I){/tex} {tex}\therefore B=\mu_{0} n I{/tex} Magnetic lines do not exist outside the body of a toroid. Toroid is closed and the solenoid is open on both sides. The magnetic field is uniform inside a toroid whereas, for a solenoid, it is different at two ends and centre. The magnetic field is made strong by, passing large current and using a laminated coil of soft iron.

Now, using Ampere's circuital law to this path, we have {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu \times 0{/tex} Therefore, B = 0 This implies, the magnetic field outside the solenoid is 0. solution_html:
1. Consider a symmetrical long solenoid having a number of turns per unit length equal to n.
    Let I be the current flowing in the solenoid, then by the right-hand rule, the magnetic field is parallel to the axis of the solenoid.

    Field inside the solenoid:
    Consider a closed path abcd.
    Now, using Ampere's circuital law to this path, we have
    {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu \times 0{/tex}
    Therefore, B = 0
    This implies, the magnetic field outside the solenoid is 0.
    Field inside the solenoid:
    Consider a closed path pqrs.
    The line integral of the magnetic field is given by,
    {tex}\oint_{\text {pqrs }} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{s p} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} ....(i)
    For path pq, {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} are along the same direction,
    {tex}\therefore \quad \int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{S p} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot d \vec{l}=\int B d l \cos 90^{\circ}{/tex} = 0
    For path rs, B = 0 because outside the solenoid field is zero.
    {tex}\therefore \quad \int_{r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex}
    Now, using Ampere's law,
    Using these equations, equation (i) gives,
    {tex}\oint_{p q r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=B l{/tex}
    Now, using Ampere's law,
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d} I=\mu_{0} I{/tex}
    This implies,
    {tex}B l=\mu_{0}(n l I){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore B=\mu_{0} n I{/tex}
  2. Magnetic lines do not exist outside the body of a toroid. Toroid is closed and the solenoid is open on both sides. The magnetic field is uniform inside a toroid whereas, for a solenoid, it is different at two ends and centre.
  3. The magnetic field is made strong by,
    1. passing large current and
    2. using a laminated coil of soft iron.

question_html:
1. An {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle, a deuteron and a proton enter into a uniform magnetic field normally with the same kinetic energy and describe circular paths. Find the ratio of radii of their paths.
  2. Give the direction of magnetic field acting on the current carrying coil ACDE shown in the figure so that the coil is in unstable equilibrium.
  3. Why do we use a low resistance ammeter in a circuit to measure current?
question_text: An {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle, a deuteron and a proton enter into a uniform magnetic field normally with the same kinetic energy and describe circular paths. Find the ratio of radii of their paths. Give the direction of magnetic field acting on the current carrying coil ACDE shown in the figure so that the coil is in unstable equilibrium. Why do we use a low resistance ammeter in a circuit to measure current?
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_\alpha v_\alpha^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2 {/tex} {tex}\frac{V_\alpha}{V_p}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_d v_d^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2{/tex} {tex}\frac{v_d}{v_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Radius of the circular path r = {tex}\frac{m v}{B q} {/tex} {tex}\frac{r_\alpha}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} & {tex}\frac{r_d}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} r a : r d : r p = 1 : {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} : 1 Magnetic field is along Z axis Low resistance ammeter does not affect the net current drawn from the battery.
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_\alpha v_\alpha^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2 {/tex} {tex}\frac{V_\alpha}{V_p}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_d v_d^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2{/tex} {tex}\frac{v_d}{v_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Radius of the circular path r = {tex}\frac{m v}{B q} {/tex} {tex}\frac{r_\alpha}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} & {tex}\frac{r_d}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} r a : r d : r p = 1 : {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} : 1 Magnetic field is along Z axis Low resistance ammeter does not affect the net current drawn from the battery.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_\alpha v_\alpha^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2 {/tex}
    {tex}\frac{V_\alpha}{V_p}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    Now,
    {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_d v_d^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{v_d}{v_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
    Radius of the circular path
    r = {tex}\frac{m v}{B q} {/tex}
    {tex}\frac{r_\alpha}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} & {tex}\frac{r_d}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} ra : rd : rp = 1 : {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} : 1
  2. Magnetic field is along Z axis
  3. Low resistance ammeter does not affect the net current drawn from the battery.

A proton P travels with a speed v, parallel to the wire, at a distance d from it in a direction opposite to the current as shown in Fig. What is the force experienced by the proton and what is its direction?
explanation: Let the conductors PQ and RS carry currents I 1 I 2 and in same direction and placed at separation r.(fig.). Consider a current–element ‘ab’ of length {tex}\Delta{/tex} L of wire RS. The magnetic field produced by current-carrying conductor PQ at the location of other wire RS {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...(i) According to Maxwell’s right hand rule the direction of B 1 will be perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed downward. Due to this magnetic field, each element of other wire experiences a force. The direction of current element is perpendicular to the magnetic field; therefore the magnetic force on element ab of length {tex}\Delta{/tex} L {tex}\Delta{/tex} F = B 1 I 2 {tex}\Delta{/tex} L sin 90 o = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r} I_2{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} L {tex}\therefore{/tex} The total force on conductor of length L will be F = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r} \Sigma{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} L {tex}\therefore{/tex} Force acting on per unit length of conductor f = {tex}\frac{F}{L}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} N/m ...(ii) Definition S.I. unit of Current (Ampere): Thus 1 ampere is the current which when flowing in each of parallel conductors placed at separation 1 m in vacuum exert a force of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 on 1m length of either wire. Magnetic field due to current carrying wire is perpendicular to plane of paper - downward. i.e., {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d} \hat{k}{/tex} Force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} = q {tex}\vec{v} \times \vec{B}{/tex} = e(-v {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left(-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d} \hat{k}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 e v I}{2 \pi d}{/tex} {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} That is the magnetic force has magnitude {tex}\frac{(\mu _0evI)}{2\pi d}{/tex} and is directed along positive x-axis i.e., in the plane of paper perpendicular to direction of vector v and to the right.

i.


We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex}
r2 = x2 + R2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil.
Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \cos \theta{/tex}
= {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex}

Magnitude of magnetic field at the enter of the coil: B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 R}{/tex}
B = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{2} \times 1}{2 \times 10^{-1}}{/tex}
= 2{tex}\pi\times{/tex} 10-4
= 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4T
